article.title: NASA Begins Fueling SLS Rocket for Artemis II, the First Crewed Lunar Mission in 53 Years
article.summary: Four astronauts are hours from liftoff aboard the SLS rocket at Kennedy Space Center, targeting a 6:24 p.m. EDT launch for a ten-day flight around the Moon.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket began taking on 756,000 gallons of super-cold propellant at Kennedy Space Center on the morning of April 1 as the agency entered the final hours of its countdown toward Artemis II, the first crewed mission beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 returned from the Moon in December 1972. Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson [authorized tanking at 7:33 a.m. EDT](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/), and liftoff is targeted for 6:24 p.m. EDT from Launch Complex 39B within a two-hour window.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) on the crew's arrival at Kennedy Space Center on March 27.

## What We Know

The four-person crew -- Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen of the Canadian Space Agency -- began their launch day at 9:25 a.m. EDT with a wake-up call, according to [NASA's live blog](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/). They are scheduled to don their orange Orion Crew Survival System suits around 12:50 p.m. before traveling to the launch pad aboard the Astrovan II, [CBS News reported](https://www.cbsnews.com/live-updates/nasa-artemis-ii-launch/).

Fueling operations progressed through multiple stages over the course of the morning. The launch team loaded approximately 537,000 gallons of liquid hydrogen and 196,000 gallons of liquid oxygen into the core stage, plus 17,000 gallons of liquid hydrogen and 5,000 gallons of liquid oxygen into the Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage upper stage, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/live-updates/nasa-artemis-ii-launch/). By late morning, the team had moved into the replenish phase, the final step in fueling that maintains propellant levels as the cryogenic fluids boil off.

Weather conditions appear cooperative. The 45th Weather Squadron projected an 80 percent probability of favorable launch conditions, with light winds, mostly clear skies, and temperatures in the mid-70s. The risk along the ascent corridor was assessed at just 9 percent, with meteorologists describing conditions as "very go" despite the possibility of isolated showers, according to [Spaceflight Now](https://spaceflightnow.com/2026/04/01/live-coverage-nasa-to-launch-artemis-2-its-first-moon-bound-mission-with-astronauts-since-1972/).

No technical holds or anomalies had been reported as of midday.

## Mission Profile

Artemis II is a roughly ten-day test flight designed to verify the Orion spacecraft's life support, navigation, communications, and propulsion systems with a crew aboard for the first time. The SLS rocket, generating approximately 8.8 million pounds of thrust at liftoff, will place Orion -- named "Integrity" for this mission -- into a low Earth orbit roughly 90 minutes after launch, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

After approximately 24 hours of systems checks in Earth orbit, the crew will perform a trans-lunar injection burn to begin a four-day coast toward the Moon. Orion will pass within 4,100 miles of the lunar surface before a free-return trajectory -- relying on the Moon's gravity rather than an engine burn -- brings the crew back toward Earth. The capsule's 16.5-foot heat shield will face temperatures approaching 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit during reentry, a critical test after Artemis I's uncrewed flight in 2022 revealed unexpected heat shield erosion, as [CBS News reported](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). Splashdown is planned for April 10 in the Pacific Ocean.

## Historic Firsts

The mission carries several records. Glover will become the first person of color to travel beyond low Earth orbit. Koch will be the first woman to do so. Hansen will be the first non-American citizen to leave Earth orbit and approach the Moon. The crew is also expected to reach a distance of 252,000 miles from Earth, surpassing the record set by Apollo 13 in 1970, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Hansen acknowledged the risks during pre-launch remarks. "You can lose a crew," he said, before adding that "the most likely outcome is we'll all be fine," as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## What We Don't Know

Whether the launch will proceed on time remains uncertain until the final minutes of the countdown. The SLS has a history of scrubbed launch attempts -- the uncrewed Artemis I mission in 2022 was delayed multiple times by hydrogen leaks and other technical issues before finally launching in November of that year. Backup launch dates extend through April 6, with the next available window roughly four weeks later if the current opportunity is missed.

The performance of Orion's heat shield at its revised entry angle -- adjusted after the erosion issues discovered on Artemis I -- remains one of the mission's primary unknowns. NASA engineers redesigned the reentry trajectory to reduce thermal stress, but the fix has not yet been validated with a crew aboard.

## Analysis

NASA Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya framed the launch in terms that extended well beyond a single mission. The vehicle, systems, and crew "are ready," he said, describing Artemis II as the start of "a campaign: landings, a lunar base, nuclear propulsion into deep space," according to [Spaceflight Now](https://spaceflightnow.com/2026/04/01/live-coverage-nasa-to-launch-artemis-2-its-first-moon-bound-mission-with-astronauts-since-1972/). That campaign now envisions an orbital test flight on Artemis III in 2027 and the first crewed lunar surface landing on Artemis IV in 2028, following the program overhaul NASA announced in late February.

The launch also arrives against the backdrop of an accelerating space race. China has publicly targeted a crewed lunar landing by 2030, and the roughly $20 billion invested in the Artemis program over seven years reflects a strategic calculation that maintaining human presence beyond low Earth orbit is a national priority, as [CBS News noted](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).
